topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, her breath catching in a shallow, uneven rhythm. The headline, the carefully crafted analysis she had poured weeks into, was undeniably hers. But the byline beneath it—a name that wasn't hers, a name belonging to a complete stranger—made the blood drain from her face. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling so violently she had to press them flat against the desk to steady them. "No," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet office. She clicked the link to the original publication, her eyes darting back and forth across the text, searching for any misplaced signature, any ghost of her own contribution. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in her chest.

[story 2]

Mark leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. He reread the final proof one more time, his gaze snagging on the attribution. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling his shoulders up toward his ears. He slowly pushed the monitor away, the sudden movement jerky. He picked up his lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic feeling suddenly heavy and foreign in his grasp. He took a long, slow sip, trying to force his mind to process the absurdity. He tapped his pen against his lower lip, a nervous habit he hadn't realized he possessed. *This can’t be right,* he thought, the conviction wavering like smoke.

[story 3]

When the email landed, Liam didn't immediately open it. He reread the subject line three times, his jaw clenching so tightly a muscle twitched near his temple. Finally, he clicked. The words swam before his eyes, the familiar arguments and insights he had painstakingly developed suddenly belonging to another person. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his posture rigid. A low, involuntary sound escaped his throat—a sort of choked exhale. He slowly pushed his chair back, the scraping noise echoing loudly in the empty workspace. He stood up, pacing the length of the room twice before stopping abruptly, staring blankly at the wall, his hands flexing and unflexing at his sides.
